text: The distal pancreatectomy is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of pancreas cancer when the tumor is located in the tail of the pancreas. During the procedure, we remove the tail of the pancreas. Due to the proximity of the spleen, as well as the need to remove lymph nodes in this area, we also remove the spleen at the same time.

At TriHealth, we perform this surgery minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is of a real distal pancreatectomy procedure performed at TriHealth. The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical distal pancreatectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how the surgery is performed. If

The surgeon lifts the stomach to access the lesser sac, using a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand to grasp and cauterize tissue. The greater omentum, a fatty layer hanging off the stomach, is divided, and blood vessels are cauterized as the bipolar energy passes between the metal rings of the instrument.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the right hand, which employs bipolar energy to cauterize and seal blood vessels, followed by a blade that divides the tissue. The stomach is lifted to access the pancreas, located retroperitoneally behind the stomach. The surgeon divides the layer of fat hanging off the stomach, extending the dissection up to the diaphragm.

The assistant pulls down on the fat layer to create tension, allowing the surgeon to divide it as they progress up the stomach towards the esophagus. The spleen, visible as a purple organ, appears in the background.

The surgeon identifies the spleen, which will be removed along with the pancreatic specimen. The proximity of the spleen to the pancreas necessitates its removal, as the splenic artery and vein are attached to the back of the pancreas. The removal ensures complete excision of surrounding lymph nodes and prevents the spleen from losing its blood supply.

The surgeon carefully dissects the connective tissue between the stomach and the superior pole of the spleen to avoid injury to the stomach. The splenic artery is visible pulsing at the bottom right of the screen, located at the superior border of the pancreas. The stomach is lifted to create tension on the tissue, facilitating the division of attachments between the posterior side of the stomach and the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon has completely mobilized the stomach, lifting it to expose the pancreas. The pancreas is visible, covered by a layer of fat, with a distinct texture and color compared to surrounding fat. The left gastric artery and vein, or left gastric pedicle, are lifted at the superior border of the pancreas. The surgeon defines the superior border of the pancreas, with the pancreas located below the cutting line and fat above it, containing lymph nodes targeted for removal in the cancer operation.

There are really two major types of pancreas cancer. The most common, or the one that you hear the most about, is pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and that's in ninety-five percent of pancreas cancers, but the one that's less common is pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, and that's only one to three percent of pancreas cancers, and fortunately that's a much less aggressive type of cancer.

The surgeon carefully dissects the left gastric artery and vein, ensuring they remain intact to maintain blood supply to the stomach. The hepatic artery is identified, and a lymph node situated over the artery is visible. The surgeon removes the lymph node from the hepatic artery to check for cancer spread.

The surgeon traces the coronary vein towards its insertion on the portal vein, then removes a lymph node for pathological examination. The pancreas is visible along with the hepatic and left gastric arteries. The surgeon begins defining the inferior border of the pancreas, preparing for a distal pancreatectomy to remove the tail of the pancreas. The splenic artery and vein are located behind the pancreas, requiring elevation of the organ for access.
The surgeon exposes the fourth portion of the duodenum, visible below the surgical field, and identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) adjacent to it.

The surgeon repositions the graspers to re-expose the pancreas, lifting it to reveal the underside. The bluish-purple splenic vein is visible beneath the pancreas, and the splenic artery is identified nearby. The surgeon prepares to divide these splenic vessels while lifting the pancreas off them.

The surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing the left gastric vessels tented up by the retractor on the left side of the screen. They proceed to remove lymphatic tissue and lymph nodes located above the pancreas, extending towards the diaphragm. Fatty tissue between the superior border of the pancreas and the left cruce of the diaphragm is being dissected.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery at its base and begins to divide it, carefully separating surrounding lymph nodes and fatty tissue. The hook cautery is used in the right hand to dissect and cauterize tissue around the artery, ensuring precise control during the procedure.

The surgeon completes dissection around the origin of the splenic artery, placing two clips on the side that remains in the body. Scissors are then used to cut the artery, ensuring secure ligation before proceeding.
The surgeon prepares to divide the pancreas by clearing space to position a stapler between the pancreas and the splenic vein, which is located far behind the pancreas.

The surgeon uses umbilical tape to lift the pancreas, creating space to pass a stapler behind it. An ultrasound is performed to locate the tumor, confirming its position in the tail of the pancreas, ensuring safe stapler placement. The stapler, equipped with a seam guard, is fired, leaving the cloth-like material in place, which is then divided with scissors. The remaining visible structure is the vein, which is yet to be addressed.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein and uses a hook cautery to sever the remaining attachments. Clips are placed on the vein, with two clips on the side remaining in the patient and one on the specimen side, before dividing the vein. The surgeon then separates a veil of tissue behind the pancreas, following it to the spleen, and detaches the spleen's attachments to free the entire specimen.

The surgeon lifts the tail of the pancreas and uses a vessel sealer to bluntly separate and divide the tissue behind the pancreas. The instrument is also used to cauterize the tissue, ensuring there is no bleeding when in the correct plane.

The surgeon lifts the pancreas and spleen, placing them into an endo-pouch bag. The spleen is positioned first, followed by the pancreas. The bag is closed, and a small incision is made to remove the bag containing the specimen, completing the robotic distal pancreatectomy.
